questions.question: State Hall's Marriage Theorem and explain its necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of a perfect matching in a bipartite graph.
keyPoints: Hall's theorem: a bipartite graph (U,V,E) has a perfect matching iff for every subset S⊆U, |N(S)|≥|S|
Equivalence to existence of a matching that covers all vertices in U (and V if |U|=|V|)
Proof outline uses augmenting paths and exchange arguments

questions.question: Describe Kuhn's algorithm (the augmenting‑path algorithm) for finding a maximum matching in a bipartite graph, and give its time complexity.
keyPoints: Start with an empty matching, repeatedly search for an augmenting path using DFS/BFS
If an augmenting path is found, flip the edges along the path to increase the matching size
Continue until no augmenting path exists – the matching is maximum
Time complexity O(V·E) in the worst case (often called O(VE) or O(nm))

questions.question: Let G = (U ∪ V, E) be a bipartite graph. (a) State König's theorem relating minimum vertex cover to maximum matching. (b) Explain how to compute a minimum vertex cover from a maximum matching in polynomial time. (c) Apply the method to the following sample bipartite graph (provide a small example).
keyPoints: König's theorem: in any bipartite graph, size of a minimum vertex cover equals size of a maximum matching
Construct the residual graph using the edges of a maximum matching
Run BFS/DFS from all unmatched vertices in U to find the set Z of reachable vertices

questions.question: Explain the Hopcroft–Karp algorithm for computing a maximum matching in a bipartite graph, highlighting the use of layered graphs, BFS to compute distances, and DFS to find vertex‑disjoint augmenting paths. Provide pseudo‑code and analyse its time complexity.
keyPoints: BFS builds distance layers from free (unmatched) vertices in U; vertices at even distance form the layered network
Only shortest augmenting paths (of minimum length d) are used in each phase
DFS searches vertex‑disjoint augmenting paths that respect the layered labeling, augmenting all found paths
Overall time O(√V·E) (or O(E√V)), significantly faster than O(VE) for dense graphs
